model_narrative: We plan a distribution network. We decide which warehouses to open, which shipment arcs from warehouses to regions to activate, and how much of each good to ship along those arcs to serve the regions. Opening a warehouse incurs a one-time fixed cost, and every unit shipped incurs a per-unit transport cost that depends on the warehouse and region. The objective is to minimize the total cost, which combines the fixed opening costs with the transportation costs across all goods.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the set of candidate warehouses that may be opened

sets.name: j
sets.doc: the set of demand regions that must be served

sets.name: k
sets.doc: the set of goods that are produced, shipped, and demanded

params.name: f
params.doc: the fixed cost of opening a warehouse, in dollars, charged once if that warehouse is opened

params.name: t
params.doc: the transport cost per unit shipped from a warehouse to a region, in dollars per unit; indexed with the region first and the warehouse second

params.name: ck
params.doc: the capacity of a warehouse for a given good, in units; the most of that good the warehouse can hold and ship out in total

params.name: dk
params.doc: the demand of a region for a given good, in units, that must be met

vars.name: totcost
vars.doc: the total cost of the solution, in dollars

vars.name: fcost
vars.doc: the total fixed cost of all opened warehouses, in dollars

vars.name: tcost
vars.doc: the total transportation cost incurred for a given good, in dollars

vars.name: ow
vars.doc: warehouse-open indicator; equals 1 if the warehouse is opened and 0 otherwise

vars.name: oa
vars.doc: shipment-arc indicator; equals 1 if the arc from the warehouse to the region is opened so that shipments may flow on it, and 0 otherwise

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the quantity of a good shipped from a warehouse to a region, in units

constraints.name: totcost
constraints.intent: Set the total cost equal to the fixed opening cost plus the transportation cost summed over all goods.

constraints.name: fcost
constraints.intent: Set the total fixed cost equal to the sum of the opening costs of the warehouses that are opened, charging each warehouse's fixed cost only when that warehouse is open.

constraints.name: tcost[k]
constraints.intent: For each good, set its transportation cost equal to the total over all warehouse and region pairs of the per-unit transport cost times the quantity of that good shipped from the warehouse to the region.

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: For each warehouse and each good, the total quantity of that good shipped out of the warehouse to all regions must not exceed the warehouse's capacity for that good.

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: For each region and each good, the total quantity of that good shipped into the region from all warehouses must be at least the region's demand for that good.

constraints.name: oa[i,
constraints.intent: For each region, at most two shipment arcs into that region may be active, so each region is served by no more than two warehouses.

constraints.name: ow[i]
constraints.intent: A shipment arc from a warehouse to a region may be active only if that warehouse is open. For each warehouse and region, the arc indicator cannot exceed the warehouse-open indicator.

constraints.name: ck[i,
constraints.intent: Shipments may flow on an arc only if that arc is active. For each warehouse, region, and good, the quantity shipped on the arc must not exceed the warehouse's capacity for that good when the arc is active, and must be zero when the arc is not active.

query: Capital approval is tight this year and there's talk of holding the line to three open warehouses instead of all four. If we cap the number of sites we actually open at three, what does the network end up costing?
expected_pyomo: model.atmost3 = Constraint(expr=sum(model.ow[i] for i in model.i) <= 3)

query: Operations is floating a two-part loading rule for good k1: keep warehouse w1's total k1 shipped out across all regions under 40 cases so its dock doesn't bottleneck, and at the same time make sure w2 carries at least 15 cases of k1 into the two closest markets, r1 and r2, combined. If we put both of those in at once, what total cost does the optimizer come back with?
expected_pyomo:
model.cap_w1k1 = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x['w1', j, 'k1'] for j in model.j) <= 40)
model.split_w2k1 = Constraint(expr=model.x['w2', 'r1', 'k1'] + model.x['w2', 'r2', 'k1'] >= 15)

query: For simpler dispatch we're toying with a strict single-source rule — each region served by exactly one warehouse rather than the up-to-two it allows today. If every region draws from just one site, where does the total cost settle?
expected_pyomo: model.single_source = Constraint(model.j, rule=lambda m, j: sum(m.oa[i, j] for i in m.i) <= 1)

query: Warehouse w1 looks like it's doing an awful lot of the heavy lifting on good k1 — its total k1 shipped out across every region it serves is piled high. Shouldn't we hold w1's combined k1 outflow to all regions down to no more than 30 cases so we're not over-leaning on that one bay? What's the model seeing that makes loading w1 up like that the cheaper call?
expected_pyomo: model.cap_w1k1_30 = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x['w1', j, 'k1'] for j in model.j) <= 30)

query: It bugs me that w2 sits comparatively idle on good k1 while w1 hauls the bulk of it. Wouldn't it be more balanced to insist w2 carry at least as much total k1 as w1 does across all regions, so the two sites share that good more evenly? What's the tradeoff that keeps the model from levelling them out?
expected_pyomo: model.w2_carry_k1 = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x['w2', j, 'k1'] for j in model.j) >= sum(model.x['w1', j, 'k1'] for j in model.j))

query: Both w1 and w2 are flagged active into region r3 in the current plan, which means r3 is leaning on two separate bays for the same deliveries. Couldn't we keep at most one of those two warehouses wired up to r3 to simplify that lane? What's pulling the optimizer toward keeping both of them open into r3?

query: Right now the plan lets a single warehouse push a huge chunk of good k1 out the door while others coast. Shouldn't every warehouse face the same ceiling — say no site ships more than 50 cases of k1 across all the regions it serves — so the k1 load is spread instead of concentrated? What's the model weighing that makes piling k1 onto one bay the better play?
expected_pyomo:
def _cap(model, i): return sum(model.x[i, j, 'k1'] for j in model.j) <= 50
model.k1_cap_each = Constraint(model.i, rule=_cap)
